items.q: What is the time complexity of binary search?
items.misconception: O(n) - because it searches through items.
items.why: Students conflate 'fast' with O(1). Binary search halves the search space each step, giving logarithmic time - much better than linear, but not constant.

items.correct: Bundling data and methods together while restricting direct access to internal state.
items.random_wrong: Encrypting data so it cannot be read.
items.why: Students take 'capsule' too literally. Encapsulation combines two ideas: keeping data and behaviour TOGETHER, and HIDING implementation behind a public interface.

items.q: What is the difference between an IP address and a MAC address?
items.correct: MAC is a hardware identifier (fixed); IP is a logical network address (assignable).
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: IP is for input, MAC is for output.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. MAC is burned into the network card (Layer 2); IP is assigned by the network (Layer 3). Both are needed to route packets to a device.
Correct answer: ALU. “MAR” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: PC. “ACC” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many registers explicitly named (PC, MAR, MDR, IR, ACC)?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
